perturbation.original_text: "Lead-time" is defined as the amount of time between Manufacturer receiving an order and EMV receipt of the goods ordered. The ordering processes are listed in Section 4.
perturbation.changed_text: "Lead-time" refers to the project lifecycle outlined in Exhibit B.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition of 'Lead-time' is specific to the time between order receipt and goods delivery, referencing Section 4 for ordering processes. The altered definition broadly ties 'Lead-time' to the 'project lifecycle outlined in Exhibit B', which details project activities and milestones rather than order fulfillment. This creates an in-text contradiction because Section 4 still refers to lead times in the context of purchase orders and delivery dates, which now conflicts with the new definition that redirects to project milestones, thus creating uncertainty for the 'Lead-time', the section that is refering the term now doesn't make sense with the new definition. Because of the change there might be an impact on '4.3 Manufacturer Assessment' or '4.2 Purchase Orders'
perturbation.contradicted_text: Based on the Forecast, EMV and Manufacturer shall meet at least quarterly to set and update mutually agreeable key performance targets in a variety of areas including, without limitations, annual pricing, Lead-time, quality and on-time delivery. EMV shall evaluate Manufacturer's performance against such targets and the parties shall agree corrective actions.
perturbation.justification: The original text defines "Lead-time" as the duration between order receipt and goods delivery, which aligns with the contradicted text's use of "Lead-time" in the context of performance targets, annual pricing, quality, and on-time delivery. The changed text redefines "Lead-time" to refer to the project lifecycle outlined in Exhibit B. This introduces a contradiction because the contradicted text uses "Lead-time" in the context of supply chain metrics, which is now incompatible with the new definition focusing on project activities and milestones. This creates a strong in-text contradiction because the term's meaning shifts from order fulfillment to project management, causing confusion and impacting the enforceability of the agreement on key performance indicators related to supply chain operations. This adheres to the definition of 'Omissions - In Text Contradiction' as it removes the original definition of 'Lead-time', creating a contradiction with obligations or constraints stated in other sections.

perturbation.original_text: Manufacturer will invoice EMV for Products net ten (10) days from when the parties sign the Quality Evaluation Form to confirm delivery of Products.
perturbation.changed_text: Manufacturer will invoice EMV for Products as per mutually agreed payment milestones.
perturbation.explanation: The original text specifies a clear invoicing timeline (net ten (10) days from signing the Quality Evaluation Form). The modified text replaces this with a vague reference to 'mutually agreed payment milestones'. While seemingly flexible, this omission creates an in-text contradiction because later sections still assume the original invoicing timeline, impacting cash flow projections and creating uncertainty about when payments are due. The change to the invoicing procedure makes the terms less defined and creates a contradiction as the original text reffers to the Quality Evaluation Form and a set period of days for invoicing. Because of the change there might be an impact on '5.1.3 EMV shall pay 70% of total amount of a Purchase Order within ten (10) days of receipt of Manufacturer's invoice as provided in Section 5.1.2 above.'
perturbation.contradicted_text: EMV shall pay 70% of total amount of a Purchase Order within ten (10) days of receipt of Manufacturer's invoice as provided in Section 5.1.2 above.
perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces the original, well-defined invoicing timeline (net ten (10) days from signing the Quality Evaluation Form) with the vague concept of 'mutually agreed payment milestones'. This omission creates a direct conflict with the contradicted text, 'EMV shall pay 70% of total amount of a Purchase Order within ten (10) days of receipt of Manufacturer's invoice as provided in Section 5.1.2 above.' The contradicted text relies on a clear invoicing procedure, which is now undefined by the modification. The phrase 'as provided in Section 5.1.2 above' no longer has a clear referent, making it difficult to determine when payment is due. This creates a strong in-text contradiction as the sections are no longer compatible and the EMV's obligation is unclear. This aligns with the 'Omissions' - In Text Contradiction definition.

perturbation.original_text: Either party may terminate this Agreement in the event of a material breach of the Agreement provided such breach is not remedied within _sixty_ (_60_) calendar days following delivery of notice of such breach.
perturbation.changed_text: Either party may terminate this Agreement in the event of a material breach of the Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The original text specifies a 60-day period to remedy a breach. By removing this clause, the agreement now lacks a specific timeframe for rectification, creating ambiguity. This omission contradicts the implicit expectation of a cure period, potentially leading to immediate termination upon any breach. In addition, the change makes the breach term for termination less defined and subjective. Because of the change there might be an impact on '8.4 Effect of Termination' or '10.9 Force Majeure'
perturbation.contradicted_text: Upon the termination of this Agreement by either party: (i) the rights and licenses granted to Manufacturer pursuant to this Agreement (including, without limitation the right to manufacture) will automatically cease; (ii) all payments owing from EMV to Manufacturer shall become immediately due and payable upon termination; (iii) all EMV trademarks, marks, trade names, patents, copyrights, designs, drawings, formulae or other data, photographs, samples, literature, and sales aids of every kind shall remain the property of EMV; and (iv) within sixty (_60_) business days after the termination of this Agreement, Manufacturer shall prepare all such items in its possession for shipment, as EMV may direct, at EMV's expense. Manufacturer shall not make or retain any copies of any confidential items or information which may have been entrusted to it.
perturbation.justification: The original text provided a 60-day period to remedy a breach, which aligns with the contradicted text's 60-day period for the Manufacturer to prepare items for shipment after termination. The changed text eliminates the cure period, potentially allowing for immediate termination upon any breach. This creates a direct contradiction with the 60-day timeframe specified in the 'Contradicted Text' because the contradicted text assumes that the Manufacturer will have 60 days to prepare the items in their possession for shipment after the contract is terminated. Without the cure period, the termination can happen faster than the 60 days the manufacturer has to prepare for shipment. This constitutes an 'Omissions - In Text Contradiction' as essential language was removed, creating a contradiction with constraints stated in other sections.
